content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814
September 14, 1976


R. J. Ballotti
District Superintendent
Gravenstein Union School District
3840 Twig Avenue
Sebastopol, California 94572

Dear Mr. Ballotti:

Assemblyman Keene has provided our agency with a copy of
your letter of August 18, 1976, regarding a resolution adopted
by the board of your school district. Essentially, the resolution
asks the Legislature to amend the Political Reform Act of 1974
to exempt elected school board members from the financial disclosure
provisions of Government Code Sections 87100, et seq.

It is understandable that unpaid school board members
believe it is an imposition to be compelled to disclose certain
of their private financial interests. However, under existing
law, school districts are governmental agencies; members of
boards of education as well as certain administrative personnel
within the school districts "make or participate in the making
of governmental decisions" within the meaning of the Political
Reform Act; and many of their decisions affect private financial
interests. Under these circumstances, the Act requires that
school districts adopt Conflict of Interest Codes applicable to
members and officials who are capable of affecting such financial
interests. Only those financial interests which could be affected
by the filer's official actions must be disclosed, and it is
the school district which has the first and primary responsibility
to identify these potential conflicts of interest and provide
for their disclosure. Hopefully, individuals who object to
overly broad disclosure will take an active role in the design
of each code's disclosure requirements, so that intrusion of
personal privacy will be kept at a minimum consistent with the
objective of preventing or disclosing conflicts of interest.

The Fair Political Practices Commission is required to and
has provided technical assistance to state and local agencies
in drafting codes. In particular, we have urged agencies to
avoid the easy path of imposing disclosure requirements with a
broad brush but rather to describe with as much precision as
possible the particular types of financial interests which
could be affected by official decisions and which therefore
must be disclosed.


TECHNICAL ASSISTANCE/ADMINISTRATION (916) 322-5660 • EXECUTIVE/LEGAL 322-5901 • ENFORCEMENT/CONFLICT OF INTEREST 322-6441


R. J. Ballotti
September 14, 1976
Page Two


The Political Reform Act was, of course, passed as an
initiative. Section 81012(a) of the Act provides that the Act
may be amended to further its purposes. Among those purposes
are the following:

     Assets and income of public officials which
     may be materially affected by their official
     actions should be disclosed and in appropriate
     circumstances the officials should be dis-
     qualified from acting in order that conflicts
     of interest may be avoided.

                              Gov. Code § 81002(g).

Given the circumstances, it is doubtful that the exemption of
school board members from the requirements of the Act could be
construed to be in furtherance of the purposes of the Act.

     Even assuming, however, that your proposal could be adopted
by the Legislature, and fully recognizing the right of school
board members to express their views, I believe the proposal
is without merit. School boards make decisions of great con-
sequence to their communities. Like other public officials,
school board members serve in a position of public trust, and
the people have the right to be assured that their decisions
will be made free from bias caused by their own financial
interests. Disclosure of any financial interest which could
foreseeably be affected by their official actions is a reason-
able and effective means of preventing conflicts of interest.
In recent years the California Supreme Court and the courts of
many other states have ruled that such disclosure requirements
are valid and that any consequent intrusion of the privacy of
the official is justified by the important public interest
that is served.

     I hope that this information is helpful. If you have
further questions, please call Mr. Delbert Spurlock, Chief of
our Conflicts of Interest Division, at (916) 322-6444.

                              Sincerely,

                              [signature]

                              Michael Bennett
                              Executive Director

Assemblyman

BARRY KEENE

Representing the
Redwood Empire

CHAIRMAN
ASSEMBLY HEALTH COMMITTEE
AND CHAIRMAN
SPECIAL SUBCOMMITTEE
ON
HEALTH CARE INVESTIGATIONS

JOINT COMMITTEE:
LEGISLATURE'S AUDITOR ON FEDERAL SOCIAL SECURITY
ASSESSMENTS OF 1972

FAIR ALLOCATION AND
CLASSIFICATION

BOARDS:
NATIONAL COMMISSION ON
STATE WORKMEN'S COMPENSATION
ADVISORY HEALTH COUNCIL
WILDLIFE CONSERVATION BOARD
WESTERN STATES FORESTRY
TASK FORCE

August 31, 1976

R. J. Ballotti
District Superintendent
Gravenstein Union School District
3840 Twig Avenue
Sebastopol, California 95472

Dear Mr. Ballotti:

Thank you for advising me that the Gravenstein Union
School Board has voted unanimously their opposition to the
inclusion of unpaid school board members within the require-
ments of the California Political Reform Act of 1974.

I have taken the liberty of sharing your letter with
Daniel Lowenstein, Chairman of the Fair Political Practices
Commission. And by copy of this letter, I am asking him to
reply directly to you regarding the continued need for such
reporting.

If I can be of assistance with this or other matters,
do not hesitate to contact me again.

Sincerely,

BARRY KEENE

BK:gbj
cc: Daniel Lowenstein with encl.


GRAVENSTEIN UNION SCHOOL DISTRICT - - 3840 TWIG AVENUE - - SEBASTOPOL - - CALIFORNIA

GRAVENSTEIN ELEMENTARY SCHOOL
3840 TWIG AVENUE
SEBASTOPOL, CALIFORNIA 95472

HILLCREST ELEMENTARY SCHOOL
725 BLOOMFIELD ROAD
SEBASTOPOL, CALIFORNIA 95472

August 18, 1976

The Honorable Barry Keene
Assemblyman, Second District
Room 5119
State Capitol Building
Sacramento, Calif.    95814

Dear Assemblyman Keene:

The Gravenstein Union School Board has voted
unanimously their opposition to the inclusion of
unpaid school board members within the requirements
of the California "Political Reform Act of 1974".

We urge you to take the necessary action to
bring about the changes needed to remove school
board members from this Act. This legislation is
an infringement on the rights of private citizens
and an invasion of personal privacy of board mem-
bers and their immediate families.

It is our feeling that compliance with the
requirements of the California "Political Reform
Act of 1974" will result in the resignation of
many board members whose qualifications and at-
tributes are needed to guide school districts
through these difficult times. Certainly there
are ample public laws already required that ade-
quately protect our citizens and taxpayers from
any conflict of interest.

Anything you can do to help resolve the
above stated problem will be appreciated.

Sincerely,

R.J. Ballotti
Dist. Superintendent

RJB/jmm
